title: Customer-Led Service Design
summary: User journey flows, heuristic audits, and hi-fi wireframes for a full Quick Service Restaurant experience.

hero.subtitle: Redesigning the end-to-end ordering journey for a national quick-service restaurant chain.
hero.description: A 9-week engagement to audit, benchmark, and reimagine a QSR's digital ordering experience — unifying mobile, kiosk, drive-thru, and in-store touchpoints into one consistent, forward-looking customer journey.

phases.title: Empathize
phases.description:
The engagement started with a full current-state audit of the client's existing mobile app and ordering experience. Rather than proposing changes based on assumption, the goal was to build an evidence-based picture of where the experience was breaking down for customers today.

I led a 10-point heuristic audit against Nielsen's usability principles — evaluating visibility of system status, user control and freedom, error prevention, consistency, recognition over recall, and more — surfacing specific friction points across the app's core flows. In parallel, I ran a competitive and market-standards audit, benchmarking the client's ordering experience against other major QSR brands' mobile, kiosk, and drive-thru models, and conducted on-site, ethnographic store visits to observe real customer and crew behavior first-hand.

phases.title: Define
phases.description: Findings from the audits and store visits were synthesized into a "Wall of Awesome" — a curated collection of the sharpest insights and opportunity areas pulled from research, used to align the client team around what mattered most before any design work began.
phases.hmw: How might we redesign the ordering experience so mobile, kiosk, and drive-thru feel like one consistent journey — instead of three disconnected systems — while preparing the brand for where ordering technology is headed next?

phases.title: Ideate
phases.description:
With the core problem defined, I mapped the full current-state customer journey end to end — Enter Restaurant, Approach Order Station, Place Order, Get Food, and Post-Visit Experience — documenting exactly where customers today run into friction at each stage, across counter, kiosk, and mobile.

Alongside the current-state maps, I designed the future-state journey for each stage, incorporating emerging ordering technologies and a more consistent visual and interaction language across every touchpoint — so a customer ordering on a kiosk, in the app, or at the drive-thru would recognize the same system, the same logic, and the same brand.

phases.title: Prototype
phases.description:
I brought the new experience to life across low- and high-fidelity wireframes for each core touchpoint — mobile ordering, in-store kiosk, and drive-thru — designing the "Place Order" flow consistently across all three so the interaction pattern stayed familiar no matter which channel a customer chose.

To communicate the vision beyond static screens, I built scenario-based service design storyboards for four distinct customer journeys, illustrating not just the interface but the full physical-plus-digital experience — from entering the restaurant through post-visit follow-up.

phases.title: Test & Iterate
phases.description: Concepts were reviewed and refined directly with client stakeholders through a series of working sessions and presentations. Over the course of the engagement, my role expanded beyond digital product design into spatial design — contributing hand-drawn schematics and architectural wires alongside the digital deliverables to support physical store layout recommendations for the client's "restaurant of the future" initiative.

quote: Internal client stakeholders noted that the engagement evolved beyond its original scope — from a digital product design effort into a spatial design contribution — recognizing the storyboards, schematics, and journey work as a meaningful step toward a more guest-first future experience.
